blocks.statement: I can identify f, λ, A, and T on a wave diagram, give correct units for each, and use f = 1/T to convert between frequency and period.

blocks.whatWeKnow: Radar uses radio waves around f ≈ 10⁹ Hz (gigahertz). At c = 3×10⁸ m/s, that's λ ≈ 0.3 m wavelength.
blocks.connection: Today: the vocabulary that lets us describe radar precisely. Day 3: the equation that connects them.

blocks.markdown:
## Four numbers describe any wave

Two are **SPACE** measurements, taken from a snapshot of the wave:

- **Wavelength λ** — distance from one crest to the next. Units: meters.
- **Amplitude A** — maximum displacement from rest. Units: meters (or Pa for sound pressure).

Two are **TIME** measurements, taken by watching one point oscillate:

- **Period T** — time for ONE complete cycle. Units: seconds.
- **Frequency f** — cycles per second. Units: hertz (Hz).

T and f are reciprocals of each other:

**f = 1/T** and **T = 1/f**

| Property | Type | Symbol | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | SPACE | λ | m, mm, nm |
| Amplitude | SPACE | A | m (Pa for sound) |
| Period | TIME | T | s |
| Frequency | TIME | f | Hz |

terms.definition: Distance from one crest to the next crest (or any equivalent point). A SPACE measurement. Units: m, mm, nm. Visible light ≈ 500 nm. Sound (at 440 Hz, A4) ≈ 0.78 m. Ocean swell ≈ 30 m. Radio FM ≈ 3 m.
terms.cognate: Sp. longitud de onda · Pt. comprimento de onda · HC longè vag

terms.definition: Maximum displacement from the rest position. A SPACE measurement. Units: m for transverse, Pa for sound pressure. A loud sound has bigger A than a soft sound at the same pitch. Bright light has bigger A than dim light at the same color.
terms.cognate: Sp. amplitud · Pt. amplitude · HC anplitid

terms.definition: Time for ONE complete cycle. A TIME measurement. Units: seconds (s). A 440 Hz tuning fork has T = 1/440 ≈ 0.00227 s. Earth's orbit: T = 1 year.
terms.cognate: Sp. período · Pt. período · HC peryòd

terms.definition: Number of cycles per second. Units: hertz (Hz). f = 1/T. A 440 Hz tuning fork vibrates 440 times per second. FM radio at 95.7 MHz vibrates 9.57 × 10⁷ times per second.
terms.cognate: Sp. frecuencia · Pt. frequência · HC fwekans

blocks.title: Watch out — units and reciprocals
blocks.markdown: λ is in METERS, not seconds — it's a distance. A is the MAX displacement, not crest-to-trough (full peak-to-peak is 2A). T is SECONDS PER CYCLE; f is CYCLES PER SECOND — they are reciprocals, so higher f = MORE cycles per second = shorter T. For sound: higher f = higher pitch.

blocks.title: One snapshot of a transverse wave (λ = 4 m, A = 2 m)
blocks.genPrompt: Read λ off this snapshot (crest at x = 1 m to crest at x = 5 m) and A (peak height). Which property — T — CANNOT be read from a single snapshot, and why?

blocks.instruction: Label each property — λ, A, T — on the sine wave with arrows. Then identify which label is a SPACE measurement and which is a TIME measurement.
prompts: Draw one sine wave; arrow λ crest-to-crest and A rest-to-crest.
Note: T can't be shown on a snapshot — it needs a clock.
Tag each label SPACE or TIME.

blocks.prompt: A wave has period T = 0.004 s. What's its frequency?
A tuning fork has f = 440 Hz. What's its period?
A radar transmitter operates at f = 2.4 GHz = 2.4 × 10⁹ Hz. What's its period (in seconds and nanoseconds)?
blocks.equationHint: T = 1/f — then convert seconds to nanoseconds.

blocks.title: Thousands of cycles per pulse
blocks.markdown: ONE 2.4 GHz radio pulse takes about 0.42 nanoseconds per cycle. In a 1 μs pulse, that's 2400 cycles. Day 15 will show why pulses contain THOUSANDS of cycles — for precise distance + Doppler measurement.

blocks.prompt: A wave has f = 250 Hz. What's its period? Show one line of work. Then complete the frame.
blocks.frame: f tells us ___ per second. T tells us seconds per ___. They are reciprocals, so high f means ___ T.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **25.1–25.3** (pendulums and the period, wave anatomy: crests, troughs, amplitude, wavelength, frequency, and how waves carry energy — not matter). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
